episode_summary: Guest Tom Scott joins the team to talk about strobe-induced hallucinations and epilepsy risks, followed by Paris’s historic air-powered clock network, a Perth penguin entertained with Pingu, and the tallest tree measured by climbing it and dropping a tape measure. The episode ranges from neurological effects of flashing lights to pneumatic tubes, penguin behavior and TV, and record-breaking trees and tape measures.

facts.fact: The first reference I can find is sort of early 19th century and they got called strobescopic hallucinations. And basically you close your eyes, you flash a strobe light.
My fact is that from the 1880s until 1927, Paris had an underground clock system which ran on puffs of air.
OK, my fact this week is, after a penguin had to be isolated due to health issues, zookeepers in Perth are keeping him entertained by letting him binge watch Pingu.
And my fact this week is that the person who measured the world's tallest tree did so by climbing to the top of it and dropping a tape measure down.
